title: Ecclesiastical Vowels
intro: The pronunciation of Latin used in the Roman Church differs from the Classical pronunciation taught in universities. Ecclesiastical Latin is the living tongue of the liturgy; it is how the saints prayed, and how the Mass is sung today.

sections.label: The Five Vowels
sections.html:
A; always "ah" as in father, never "ay".
E; always "eh" as in bed, never "ee".
I; always "ee" as in machine, never "eye".
O; always "oh" as in note, never "uh".
U; always "oo" as in food, never "yoo".

sections.label: Nota Bene · Take Note
sections.html: The key rule: Latin vowels are pure. English speakers diphthongise (glide between sounds); in Latin, hold each vowel steady. "Amen" is AH-men, not AY-men.

sections.label: Practica · Practice
sections.note: Tap each word to reveal pronunciation and meaning.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Latin vowels are pure and stable: A = ah, E = eh, I = ee, O = oh, U = oo. Never diphthongise. Practise with common prayer words until the sounds feel natural.

title: Consonant Rules
intro: Most Latin consonants sound as in English; but a handful follow rules that transform familiar words. Master these, and every Latin prayer opens before you.

sections.label: The Key Rules
sections.html:
C; hard (k) before a, o, u, or a consonant (caelum = CHEH-loom wait; before e/i it softens to ch).
C + e/i; soft "ch" as in church (Cǽlum = CHEH-loom).
G + e/i; soft "j" as in gem (regina = reh-JEE-nah).
Gn; "ny" as in canyon (Agnus = AH-nyoos).
Sc + e/i; "sh" (suscipe = SOO-shee-peh).
Ti + vowel; "tsee" (gratia = GRAH-tsee-ah).

sections.label: Mnemonic
sections.html: "Cherubim sing gently: Agnus Dei"; cherubim (ch), gently (soft g), Agnus (ny).

sections.label: Practica · Practice
sections.note: Tap each word to reveal pronunciation and meaning.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Six rules unlock nearly every Latin consonant: c/g + e/i soften, gn = ny, sc + e/i = sh, ti + vowel = tsee. Everything else is English.

title: Stress & Accent
intro: Latin words have one stressed syllable. Get the stress right and you sound fluent; get it wrong and you sound foreign. Thankfully, the rule is simple.

sections.label: The Penultimate Rule
sections.html:
The stress falls on the second-to-last syllable (penult) unless it is short; in which case it falls on the third-to-last (antepenult).

You'll see an acute accent in this app (Dómi­nus, Glória); that mark shows you exactly which syllable to stress. Follow the mark and you cannot err.

sections.label: Shortcut
sections.html: If the word has an accent mark printed above a vowel, stress THAT vowel. No guessing required.

sections.label: Practica · Practice
sections.note: Tap each word. Stress is ALL-CAPS in the phonetic.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Stress the syllable with the acute accent. When in doubt, stress the second-to-last syllable. This rule covers 95% of all Latin words.

title: First & Second Declensions
intro: Latin nouns change their endings to show their job in the sentence; subject, object, possession, direction. This is called declension. The two most common patterns give you hundreds of prayer words.

sections.label: First Declension (-a)
sections.html: First-declension nouns are usually feminine and end in -a in the nominative: María, grátia, vita, gloria, córona. The case endings follow a predictable pattern.

sections.label: Second Declension (-us / -um)
sections.html: Second-declension nouns are usually masculine (in -us) or neuter (in -um): Dóminus, Christus, Iesus, verbum, sæculum.

sections.label: Look for the Ending
sections.html: In the Mass you'll see phrases like per Dóminum nostrum (accusative; 'through our Lord') and Glória Dómini (genitive; 'glory of the Lord'). The ending tells you the meaning.

sections.label: Vocabula · Vocabulary
sections.note: Common 1st and 2nd declension nouns from the Mass.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: 1st declension: -a / -æ / -æ / -am / -a. 2nd declension: -us / -i / -o / -um / -o. These two patterns cover most nouns in prayer.

title: Verb Basics
intro: Latin verbs conjugate; they change their endings by person and number. The good news: for reading prayers, you only need the present tense and a handful of endings to understand most of what you'll encounter.

sections.label: Four Conjugation Groups
sections.html:
Latin verbs are grouped by the vowel of their stem:

I: -āre (laudáre; to praise)
II: -ēre (vidére; to see)
III: -ere (dúcere; to lead)
IV: -īre (audíre; to hear)

sections.label: The Endings Are the Key
sections.html: -o, -s, -t, -mus, -tis, -nt; learn these six endings and you can identify the subject of nearly any Latin verb. Laudámus te = 'We praise Thee' (the -mus ending IS 'we').

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Learn the six present-tense endings (-o, -s, -t, -mus, -tis, -nt) and you can decode the subject of any verb in prayer.

title: Core Prayer Vocabulary
intro: Learn these ten essential words and you will recognize over half of what you hear in the Mass and the Rosary. Start here.

sections.label: The Ten Most Common Words
sections.note: Tap to reveal meaning and an example.

sections.label: Build on These
sections.html: Dóminus + Deus + Pater + Fílius + Spíritus + Sanctus = the vocabulary of the opening of almost every prayer. Master these first.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Ten words is a huge head start. Each one appears dozens of times across the Mass, the Office, and the Rosary. Flip these cards daily for a week.

title: Master the Ave María
intro: The Hail Mary is the most-prayed prayer of the Rosary; 50 times in every chaplet, hundreds of times in a lifetime. Let's break it down phrase by phrase until every word is known.

sections.label: The Origin
sections.html: The first half comes directly from Scripture: the angel Gabriel's greeting at the Annunciation (Lk 1:28) and Elizabeth's blessing at the Visitation (Lk 1:42). The second half is the Church's response, added gradually from the 15th century.

items.lat: et benedíctus fructus ventris tui, Iesus.
items.phon: et beh-neh-DEEK-toos FROOK-toos VEN-trees TOO-ee, YEH-soos
items.eng: and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus.

items.lat: nunc et in hora mortis nostræ.
items.phon: noonk et in OH-rah MOHR-tees NOHS-treh
items.eng: now and at the hour of our death.

sections.label: Vocabula · Key Phrases
sections.note: Master each phrase of the Hail Mary.

items.eng: at the hour of our death

sections.label: Pray It Slowly
sections.html: The Rosary is not a race. Pray the Ave deliberately, letting each phrase settle. Even one Ave said with full attention is worth more than a hundred rushed.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: The Ave María has two halves: the Scriptural greeting (Ave Maria… fructus ventris tui, Iesus) and the Church's plea (Sancta Maria… in hora mortis nostræ). Each phrase is a scriptural echo.

title: Master the Pater Noster
intro: The Lord's own prayer. Every phrase came from Our Lord's own lips. Know it in Latin and you pray with the Church of every century.

sections.label: The Seven Petitions
sections.html: The Pater Noster contains seven petitions: the first three concerning God's glory (Name, Kingdom, Will), the last four concerning our needs (bread, forgiveness, temptation, evil). It is the perfect prayer.

items.lat: sicut in cælo et in terra.
items.phon: SEE-koot in CHEH-loh et in TEHR-rah
items.eng: on earth as it is in heaven.

items.lat: sicut et nos dimíttimus debitóribus nostris.
items.phon: SEE-koot et nohs dee-MEET-tee-moos deh-bee-TOH-ree-boos NOS-treess
items.eng: as we forgive those who trespass against us.

items.lat: Et ne nos indúcas in tentatiónem,
items.phon: et neh nohs in-DOO-kahs in ten-tah-tsee-OH-nem
items.eng: And lead us not into temptation,

items.lat: sed líbera nos a malo. Ámen.
items.phon: sed LEE-beh-rah nohs ah MAH-loh. AH-men
items.eng: but deliver us from evil. Amen.

sections.label: Vocabula · The Seven Petitions
sections.note: Learn each petition of the Our Father.

sections.label: Pray It Daily in Latin
sections.html: Pray the Pater Noster in Latin once a day for a week. By the end you'll have it memorized; and you'll carry the Church's prayer in the Church's tongue for life.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Seven petitions, taught by Our Lord Himself. The Pater Noster is the centre of the Mass, the Rosary, and the Divine Office. Know it in Latin.

title: Reading Latin Psalms
intro: The Psalms are the prayer book of the Church. Once you know their structure, you can read them in Latin even with modest vocabulary. The secret is parallelism.

sections.label: Hebrew Parallelism
sections.html:
Every psalm verse has two (or sometimes three) parallel halves that say the same thing in different words. Once you recognize this, the second half often explains the first.

Example from Psalm 22 (The Lord is my Shepherd):

items.lat: In loco páscuæ ibi me collocávit,
items.phon: in LOH-koh PAHS-kweh EE-bee meh kol-loh-KAH-veet
items.eng: In a place of pasture He hath set me,
He hath brought me up on the water of refreshment.

sections.label: Vocabula · Psalm Vocabulary
sections.note: Words that appear constantly in the psalms.

sections.label: Read It Aloud
sections.html: Read the Latin aloud first, slowly. Even without understanding every word, the rhythm and parallelism will carry you. Then consult the English.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: Parallelism is your key. When you don't understand the first line, look at the second; it says the same thing in different words.

title: Reading the Vulgate
intro: St. Jerome's Latin Bible; the Vulgate; is the Bible of the Church. With the grammar and vocabulary you've learned, you can now open it and read. Begin with John 1.

sections.label: John 1:1–5
sections.html: The Prologue of John is the most famous opening in Scripture; and the Last Gospel of the Latin Mass. Read it with the phrase-by-phrase breakdown below.

items.eng: In the beginning was the Word,
and the Word was with God,

items.lat: Hoc erat in princípio apud Deum.
items.phon: hok EH-raht in preen-CHEE-pee-oh AH-pood DEH-oom
items.eng: The same was in the beginning with God.
All things were made through Him,

items.lat: et sine ipso factum est nihil quod factum est.
items.phon: et SEE-neh EEP-soh FAHK-toom est NEE-heel kwohd FAHK-toom est
items.eng: and without Him was made nothing that was made.
and the life was the light of men.
And the light shineth in darkness,
and the darkness did not comprehend it.

sections.label: Vocabula · John 1 Vocabulary
sections.note: Key words from the Prologue of St. John's Gospel.

sections.label: Next Steps
sections.html: Once you can read John 1, try Psalm 50 (the Miserere) and the beginning of Genesis. The Vulgate is now yours for life.

sections.label: Summarium · Summary
sections.html: You can now read the Vulgate. St. Jerome's Latin is at your fingertips; the same Bible the saints and scholars of a thousand years prayed from.
